title: Learn German with Kedar Jadhav : B1 : Session 9 : Indefinitpronomen

study_notes.summary: This session covers Indefinitpronomen (indefinite pronouns) at the B1 level. Indefinite pronouns refer to unspecified people, things, or quantities. Key pronouns include man, jemand, niemand, etwas, nichts, alle, einige, manche, jeder, and keiner. Understanding their declension and usage is critical for Sprachbausteine cloze exercises and for natural spoken German.
study_notes.key_concept: Indefinitpronomen replace or refer to unspecified nouns. They must be declined correctly depending on their grammatical case (Nominativ, Akkusativ, Dativ). Some have optional endings (jemand/jemanden/jemandem) while others are fixed (etwas, nichts).
study_notes.grammar_rule: Declension of key indefinite pronouns: man (only Nom.), einen (Akk.), einem (Dat.); jemand → jemand(en) Akk., jemand(em) Dat.; niemand → niemand(en), niemand(em); jeder → jeden (Akk.), jedem (Dat.); keiner → keinen, keinem. etwas and nichts are invariable.
study_notes.word_order_note: Indefinitpronomen occupy the same position as regular nouns/pronouns. When used as subject: 'Jemand hat angerufen.' When as object: 'Ich kenne niemanden hier.'
common_mistakes: Using 'man' in accusative — correct form is 'einen' (Man kann einen nicht zwingen.)
Forgetting optional endings on jemand/niemand in accusative and dative
Confusing 'einige' (some specific) with 'manche' (some in general)
Using 'jeder' with plural verbs — jeder always takes singular
Mixing up 'etwas' (something) and 'ein bisschen' (a little bit)

vocabulary.example_de: Jemand hat an der Tür geklopft.

vocabulary.example_en: I would like to eat something.

vocabulary.example_de: Manche Leute stehen gern früh auf.
vocabulary.example_en: Some people like to get up early.

grammar_patterns.example_de: Man muss sich anpassen. Das kann einen verrückt machen.
grammar_patterns.example_en: One must adapt. That can drive one crazy.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Sprachbausteine cloze frequently tests 'man' vs. 'einen/einem'

grammar_patterns.structure: jemand(en) Akk., jemand(em) Dat. — endings optional in spoken German
grammar_patterns.example_de: Ich suche jemanden, der mir helfen kann.
grammar_patterns.example_en: I'm looking for someone who can help me.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Reading comprehension and Sprachbausteine — recognize both forms

grammar_patterns.example_de: Ich habe etwas Interessantes gelesen.
grammar_patterns.example_en: I read something interesting.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Common in Lesen passages and Sprachbausteine fill-in-the-blank

grammar_patterns.example_de: Jeder Schüler bekommt ein Buch.
grammar_patterns.example_en: Every student gets a book.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Subject-verb agreement tested in Sprachbausteine

flashcards.front: How do you say 'someone' in German?
flashcards.back: jemand

flashcards.front: What is the accusative of 'man'?
flashcards.back: einen (Das kann einen nervös machen.)

flashcards.front: niemand
flashcards.back: nobody, no one

flashcards.front: etwas Interessantes
flashcards.back: something interesting — after etwas/nichts, adjectives are capitalized with -es ending

flashcards.front: Does 'jeder' take a singular or plural verb?
flashcards.back: Singular — Jeder hat seine eigene Meinung.

flashcards.front: What's the difference between 'einige' and 'manche'?
flashcards.back: einige = some (specific/countable), manche = some (general tendency)

flashcards.front: keiner
flashcards.back: none, nobody — Keiner hat mich gesehen.

flashcards.front: What follows 'etwas' or 'nichts' when combined with an adjective?
flashcards.back: A nominalized adjective with capital letter and -es ending: etwas Neues, nichts Besonderes

flashcards.front: alle
flashcards.back: everyone, all — Alle waren zufrieden.

flashcards.front: Dative form of 'jemand'?
flashcards.back: jemandem (optional ending) — Ich helfe jemandem.

practice_sentences.en: Someone took my book.
I have something beautiful for you.
Every student must do the homework.
I don't know anyone here.
One should be punctual.

exam_tips.tip: In Sprachbausteine Teil A, indefinite pronouns like 'man', 'jemand', 'etwas', and 'nichts' are common answer options. Pay attention to the grammatical case required.
Remember: etwas/nichts + adjective always takes a capital letter and -es ending. This pattern appears frequently in reading passages.
When writing emails (Schreiben), use 'man' to make general statements: 'Man sollte darauf achten, dass...' This sounds more formal and appropriate.
In the oral exam, use 'jeder' and 'alle' to express opinions about groups: 'Ich denke, jeder sollte Sport treiben.'

visual_summary.title: Indefinitpronomen — Declension Overview
key_points: man → einen (Akk.), einem (Dat.) — never 'mann'!
jemand/niemand endings are optional in everyday speech
etwas/nichts + Adjektiv → capital letter + -es: etwas Neues
jeder = always singular verb; alle = always plural verb

title: Learn German with Kedar Jadhav : B1 : Session 8 : Adjektivdeklination Part 2

study_notes.summary: Part 2 of Adjektivdeklination covers adjective endings without articles (Nullartikel) and after indefinite articles (ein, kein, mein, etc.). When there is no article, the adjective takes the strong ending (showing gender/case). After ein-words, the adjective takes mixed declension endings. This is one of the most tested grammar areas in TELC B1 Sprachbausteine.
study_notes.key_concept: Without an article, the adjective must 'show' the gender/case itself (strong endings = like der/die/das endings). After ein-words, the adjective takes strong endings in Nom. masc. (-er) and Nom./Akk. neuter (-es), and weak endings (-en) elsewhere.
study_notes.grammar_rule: Nullartikel (strong): Nom: -er/-e/-es/-e(pl); Akk: -en/-e/-es/-e; Dat: -em/-er/-em/-en; Gen: -en/-er/-en/-er. Ein-words (mixed): Nom: -er/-e/-es/-en; Akk: -en/-e/-es/-en; Dat: -en/-en/-en/-en; Gen: -en/-en/-en/-en.
study_notes.word_order_note: Adjectives always come between the article (if any) and noun: kalter Kaffee, ein kalter Kaffee, der kalte Kaffee. Multiple adjectives all take the same ending: frischer, warmer Tee.
common_mistakes: Forgetting strong endings when there's no article: 'kalter Kaffee' not 'kalt Kaffee'
After 'ein' in masculine nominative: 'ein großer Mann' (not 'ein großen Mann')
After 'ein' in neuter nominative/accusative: 'ein kleines Kind' (not 'ein kleinen Kind')
In dative, all adjective forms end in -en regardless of article type
Forgetting that plural without article uses strong endings: 'deutsche Bücher'

vocabulary.example_de: Er hat ein gebrauchtes Auto gekauft.

grammar_patterns.example_de: Kalter Kaffee schmeckt nicht gut.
grammar_patterns.example_en: Cold coffee doesn't taste good.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Sprachbausteine often tests adjective endings without articles

grammar_patterns.example_de: Ein junger Mann wartet draußen.
grammar_patterns.example_en: A young man is waiting outside.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Key pattern in Sprachbausteine Teil A — -er ending for masc. Nom.

grammar_patterns.example_de: Ich möchte ein kaltes Getränk.
grammar_patterns.example_en: I'd like a cold drink.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Frequently tested — -es for neuter after ein-words

grammar_patterns.example_de: Mit frischem Brot schmeckt es besser.
grammar_patterns.example_en: With fresh bread it tastes better.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Simplifying rule: after prepositions requiring dative, always use -en ending

flashcards.front: What ending does an adjective take with no article in masculine Nominativ?
flashcards.back: -er (kalter Kaffee, deutscher Wein)

flashcards.front: What's the adjective ending after 'ein' in masculine Nominativ?
flashcards.back: -er (ein großer Mann) — same as no article

flashcards.front: What ending after 'ein' in neuter Nominativ/Akkusativ?
flashcards.back: -es (ein kleines Kind)

flashcards.front: What's the universal Dativ adjective ending?
flashcards.back: -en — for ALL article types (dem alten Mann, einem alten Mann, altem Mann)

flashcards.front: frisch
flashcards.back: fresh — Frisches Obst ist gesund.

flashcards.front: günstig
flashcards.back: affordable/cheap — Ich suche günstige Kleidung.

flashcards.front: No article, feminine Nominativ adjective ending?
flashcards.back: -e (kalte Milch, deutsche Musik)

flashcards.front: No article, plural Nominativ adjective ending?
flashcards.back: -e (kleine Kinder, gute Bücher)

flashcards.front: gemütlich
flashcards.back: cozy, comfortable — eine gemütliche Wohnung

flashcards.front: What's the trick for Dativ adjective endings?
flashcards.back: In Dativ, the adjective ALWAYS ends in -en, regardless of article type!

practice_sentences.en: I like drinking cold water.
He's wearing a new shirt.

practice_sentences.de: Mit frisch__ Obst kann man Salat machen.
practice_sentences.en: You can make salad with fresh fruit.

practice_sentences.de: Sie hat ein__ alt__ Tasche gekauft.
practice_sentences.en: She bought an old bag.
practice_sentences.hint: Feminine, eine, Akkusativ → -e: eine alte Tasche

practice_sentences.en: German beer is world-famous.

exam_tips.tip: For Sprachbausteine: When you see no article before an adjective, the adjective needs a STRONG ending that matches der/die/das. Think: 'The adjective does the article's job.'
Quick Dativ trick: ALL adjective endings in Dativ are -en, period. This alone can help you answer 30% of adjective questions correctly.
In Lesen passages, recognizing correct adjective endings helps you understand which noun the adjective belongs to, especially in complex sentences.
When writing your email (Schreiben), use adjectives to add detail: 'Ich habe eine interessante Erfahrung gemacht' shows good B1-level language.

visual_summary.title: Adjektivdeklination — Without Article & After ein-Words
key_points: No article → adjective takes STRONG ending (shows gender/case)
After ein-word: Nom. mask. (-er) and Nom./Akk. neut. (-es) are strong; rest is -en
Dativ is ALWAYS -en for every article type
Multiple adjectives before a noun all get the same ending

title: Learn German with Kedar Jadhav : B1 : Session 7 : Adjektivdeklination Part 1

study_notes.summary: Part 1 of Adjektivdeklination covers adjective endings after definite articles (der, die, das, die) and demonstrative articles (dieser, jeder, etc.). After definite articles, adjectives take weak endings: -e in Nominativ (all genders) and feminine/neuter Akkusativ, and -en everywhere else. This is the simplest of the three declension patterns.
study_notes.key_concept: After definite articles (der/die/das), the article already shows the gender and case clearly, so the adjective only needs a 'weak' ending. The weak endings are just -e or -en. Use -e for Nominativ (all genders) and Akkusativ feminine + neuter. Use -en for everything else.
study_notes.grammar_rule: Weak declension (after der/die/das/die, dieser, jeder, welcher, alle): Nom: -e/-e/-e/-en; Akk: -en/-e/-e/-en; Dat: -en/-en/-en/-en; Gen: -en/-en/-en/-en. Simple rule: -e for 5 spots in Nom/Akk, -en for the rest.
study_notes.word_order_note: The order is always: definite article + adjective + noun. 'Der alte Mann', 'die kleine Katze'. In sentences: the adjective block stays together immediately before its noun.
common_mistakes: Using -er/-es after definite articles — weak endings are only -e or -en
Confusing der (Nom. mask.) → -e: 'der alte Mann' not 'der alter Mann'
Forgetting that masculine Akkusativ changes: 'den alten Mann' (not 'den alte Mann')
All Dativ and Genitiv forms are -en — no exceptions with definite articles
Mixing up rules: after 'der' = weak (-e/-en), after 'ein' = mixed, no article = strong

vocabulary.example_de: Der alte Mann sitzt im Park.
vocabulary.example_en: The old man is sitting in the park.

vocabulary.example_de: Die junge Frau liest ein Buch.
vocabulary.example_en: The young woman is reading a book.

vocabulary.example_de: Das kleine Kind spielt im Garten.
vocabulary.example_en: The small child is playing in the garden.

vocabulary.example_de: Das interessante Buch habe ich gelesen.
Der bekannte Schauspieler kommt aus Berlin.
vocabulary.example_en: The well-known actor comes from Berlin.

grammar_patterns.example_de: Der neue Lehrer ist nett. Die große Stadt ist laut.
grammar_patterns.example_en: The new teacher is nice. The big city is loud.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Most common adjective pattern in Lesen reading passages

grammar_patterns.example_de: Ich kenne den neuen Lehrer.
grammar_patterns.example_en: I know the new teacher.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Sprachbausteine frequently tests Akkusativ adjective endings

grammar_patterns.example_de: Ich helfe dem alten Mann. Sie wohnt in der kleinen Wohnung.
grammar_patterns.example_en: I help the old man. She lives in the small apartment.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: After Dativ prepositions (mit, bei, von etc.), always -en

grammar_patterns.structure: Same pattern as definite article (weak endings)
grammar_patterns.example_de: Jeder neue Mitarbeiter bekommt eine Einführung.
grammar_patterns.example_en: Every new employee gets an introduction.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: dieser/jeder/welcher trigger the same weak endings as der/die/das

flashcards.front: What ending does an adjective get after 'der' in Nominativ?
flashcards.back: -e (der alte Mann, der neue Lehrer)

flashcards.front: What ending after 'die' in Nominativ (feminine)?
flashcards.back: -e (die kleine Katze)

flashcards.front: What ending after 'den' in Akkusativ (masculine)?
flashcards.back: -en (Ich sehe den alten Mann.)

flashcards.front: Rule for Dativ adjective endings after definite articles?
flashcards.back: Always -en! dem alten Mann, der kleinen Frau, den neuen Büchern

flashcards.front: What are the two possible weak endings?
flashcards.back: -e and -en. That's it! -e for Nom (all) + Akk (fem/neut), -en for everything else.

flashcards.front: bekannt
flashcards.back: well-known, famous — Der bekannte Autor hat ein neues Buch geschrieben.

flashcards.front: Does 'dieser' use the same adjective endings as 'der'?
flashcards.back: Yes! dieser, jeder, welcher, alle → same weak endings as definite article

flashcards.front: die langen Ferien
flashcards.back: the long holidays — Plural Nominativ after definite article: -en

flashcards.front: wichtig
flashcards.back: important — Die wichtige Nachricht kommt morgen.

flashcards.front: How many weak ending forms exist?
flashcards.back: Only two: -e and -en. Nom. sing. + Akk. fem./neut. = -e; all others = -en

practice_sentences.de: Der neu__ Nachbar ist sehr freundlich.
practice_sentences.en: The new neighbor is very friendly.

practice_sentences.de: Ich habe die interessant__ Sendung gesehen.
practice_sentences.en: I watched the interesting show.

practice_sentences.de: Er gibt dem klein__ Kind ein Geschenk.
practice_sentences.en: He gives the small child a present.

practice_sentences.de: Die alt__ Gebäude stehen unter Denkmalschutz.
practice_sentences.en: The old buildings are protected as monuments.
Do you know the famous singer?

exam_tips.tip: For Sprachbausteine: After der/die/das, you only choose between -e and -en. If it's Nominativ or Akkusativ (fem/neut), it's -e. Everything else is -en.
In Lesen texts, adjective endings tell you the case and gender. Use them as clues to understand sentence structure when vocabulary is difficult.
For Schreiben: Using adjectives with correct endings in your email shows strong B1 grammar. Try: 'die nette Kollegin', 'der interessante Vorschlag'.

visual_summary.title: Adjektivdeklination — After Definite Articles (Weak Endings)
key_points: Only 2 endings to remember: -e and -en
-e: Nominativ (all genders singular) + Akkusativ (feminine + neuter)
-en: everything else (Akk. mask., all Dativ, all Genitiv, all Plural)
Same rule applies after dieser, jeder, welcher, alle

title: Learn German with Kedar Jadhav : B1 : Session 6 : B1 Examination Structure and Tips

study_notes.summary: Session 6 provides a comprehensive overview of the TELC B1 exam structure, scoring system, and strategic preparation tips. The TELC B1 exam has two main parts: schriftliche Prüfung (written: Lesen, Hören, Sprachbausteine, Schreiben) and mündliche Prüfung (oral: Sprechen). Total 300 points, you need 60% in BOTH written and oral separately to pass.
study_notes.key_concept: The TELC B1 exam tests four skills plus Sprachbausteine. Written section = 195 points (Lesen 75 + Hören 75 + Sprachbausteine 30 + Schreiben 45 minus overlap). Oral section = 75 points. You must pass BOTH sections with at least 60% to get the certificate.
study_notes.grammar_rule: No specific grammar rule — this session focuses on exam strategy. Key structural knowledge: Lesen has 3 parts (global reading, detail MCQ, selective matching), Hören has 3 parts (global, detail, selective), Sprachbausteine has 2 parts (MCQ cloze, word bank cloze).
study_notes.word_order_note: For the Schreiben section, remember standard German letter format: Anrede (greeting), Einleitung (introduction), Hauptteil (main part addressing all 4 Leitpunkte), Schluss (closing), Grußformel (sign-off).
common_mistakes: Not reading ALL answer options in Lesen before choosing — first read the question, then scan
Trying to understand every word in Hören — focus on keywords and context
Ignoring Leitpunkte in Schreiben — you MUST address all 4 points
Not managing time — Lesen (60 min), Hören (30 min), Sprachbausteine (30 min total with Lesen)
Neglecting the oral exam prep — it's 25% of total score and must be passed separately

vocabulary.example_en: The B1 exam takes about 3 hours.

vocabulary.example_en: I want to pass the exam on the first try.

Sie müssen alle vier Leitpunkte bearbeiten.
vocabulary.example_en: You must address all four guiding points.

vocabulary.example_de: Kreuzen Sie die richtige Antwort an.
Die mündliche Prüfung findet am Nachmittag statt.
vocabulary.example_en: The oral exam takes place in the afternoon.

grammar_patterns.example_en: Read the text. Mark the correct answer.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Understanding exam instructions is crucial — they use formal imperative

grammar_patterns.example_de: Sie müssen alle vier Leitpunkte bearbeiten.
grammar_patterns.example_en: You must address all four guiding points.
grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Exam instructions use müssen to indicate mandatory requirements

grammar_patterns.pattern: Conditional with wenn for exam strategy

grammar_patterns.example_en: If you don't know a question, move on to the next one.

flashcards.front: What are the 5 sections of TELC B1?
flashcards.back: Lesen (75pts), Hören (75pts), Sprachbausteine (30pts), Schreiben (45pts), Sprechen (75pts) = 300 total

flashcards.front: What percentage do you need to pass TELC B1?
flashcards.back: 60% in BOTH written AND oral sections separately

flashcards.front: How many Leitpunkte must you address in Schreiben?
flashcards.back: All 4 — missing even one will cost significant points

flashcards.front: bestehen
flashcards.back: to pass (an exam) — Ich habe die Prüfung bestanden!

flashcards.front: What are the 3 parts of Sprechen?
flashcards.back: Teil 1: Self-introduction (3 min), Teil 2: Topic/Opinion (6 min), Teil 3: Joint Planning (6 min)

flashcards.front: der Leitpunkt
flashcards.back: guiding point — the 4 points you must address in the Schreiben email

flashcards.front: What does Sprachbausteine test?
flashcards.back: Teil A: MCQ cloze (grammar in context), Teil B: Word bank cloze (fill-in from word list)

flashcards.front: ankreuzen
flashcards.back: to mark/tick (a box) — Kreuzen Sie die richtige Antwort an.

flashcards.front: How long is the written exam?
flashcards.back: About 2.5 hours: Lesen+Sprachbausteine (90 min), Hören (30 min), Schreiben (30 min)

flashcards.front: What's the word count for Schreiben?
flashcards.back: 100-120 words (semi-formal email reply)

practice_sentences.en: The exam consists of five parts.
You must achieve at least 60%.
Read the text carefully.
The oral exam takes place in the afternoon.
All four guiding points must be addressed.

exam_tips.tip: Time management is everything: spend max 15 minutes on Lesen Teil 1, 25 minutes on Teil 2, 15 minutes on Teil 3, and 15 minutes on Sprachbausteine.
In Hören, read the questions BEFORE the audio plays. This primes your brain to listen for specific information.
For Schreiben, use a formula: Greeting → Reference to received email → Address each Leitpunkt in order → Closing. Stick to 100-120 words.
In Sprechen Teil 3 (Gemeinsam Planen), use planning phrases: 'Ich schlage vor...', 'Was hältst du davon?', 'Einverstanden, dann...'
Never leave any answer blank! Even a guess gives you a chance. In Lesen and Hören MCQ, you have a 33% chance with random guessing.

visual_summary.title: TELC B1 Exam Structure Overview

rows: Teil 1: Headlines→Texts, Teil 2: MCQ, Teil 3: Matching
Teil 1: Global, Teil 2: Detail, Teil 3: Selective
Teil A: MCQ cloze, Teil B: Word bank
Teil 1: Intro, Teil 2: Topic, Teil 3: Planning

key_points: Total: 300 points. Pass = 60% in BOTH written AND oral
Written exam: Lesen + Sprachbausteine together (90 min), then Hören (30 min), then Schreiben (30 min)
Oral exam: With a partner, 3 parts, ~15 minutes total
Key tip: Never leave blanks — always make your best guess

title: Learn German with Kedar Jadhav : B1 : Session 5 : B1 Examination Structure and Tips

study_notes.summary: Session 5 continues the examination overview with detailed focus on each exam section's strategy. Covers how to approach Lesen (reading) with skimming and scanning techniques, Hören (listening) with keyword prediction, Sprachbausteine with elimination strategy, Schreiben with template approach, and Sprechen with confidence-building techniques.
study_notes.key_concept: Each TELC B1 section requires a different strategy. For Lesen: skim first, then match. For Hören: predict keywords. For Sprachbausteine: eliminate wrong options. For Schreiben: use a template. For Sprechen: prepare stock phrases. Strategy is as important as language knowledge.
study_notes.grammar_rule: Focus on grammar patterns frequently tested in Sprachbausteine: Konnektoren (weil, obwohl, dass, wenn, damit), Präpositionen mit Kasus (wegen + Gen, trotz + Gen, mit + Dat, für + Akk), and verb conjugation patterns (Konjunktiv II, Passiv).
study_notes.word_order_note: For Nebensatz connectors tested in Sprachbausteine: After weil/obwohl/dass/wenn/damit, the verb goes to the END: 'Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.'
common_mistakes: In Lesen Teil 1: Reading every text completely instead of matching headlines by keywords
In Hören: Panicking when you miss a word — the overall context matters more
In Sprachbausteine: Not reading the ENTIRE sentence before choosing an answer
In Schreiben: Writing too much (over 120 words) or too little (under 100 words)
In Sprechen: Speaking too fast when nervous — slow and clear is better than fast and unclear

grammar_patterns.telc_relevance: Reflexive verbs with prepositions appear in both Lesen and Sprachbausteine

flashcards.front: What's the best strategy for Lesen Teil 1?
flashcards.back: Read headlines first, then skim each text for matching keywords — don't read every word

flashcards.front: überfliegen
flashcards.back: to skim (a text) — Überfliegen Sie den Text vor dem Lesen der Fragen.

flashcards.front: What's the elimination strategy for Sprachbausteine?
flashcards.back: Read the full sentence, eliminate options that are grammatically impossible, then choose from remaining

flashcards.front: ausschließen
flashcards.back: to exclude/eliminate — Schließen Sie falsche Antworten aus.

flashcards.front: How should you approach Hören?
flashcards.back: Read questions BEFORE audio plays, circle keywords, listen for those specific pieces of information

flashcards.front: der Schlüsselbegriff
flashcards.back: keyword — Markieren Sie die Schlüsselbegriffe!

flashcards.front: What template should you use for Schreiben?
flashcards.back: Greeting → Reference to email → 4 Leitpunkte (1-2 sentences each) → Closing → Sign-off

flashcards.front: sich vorbereiten auf + ?
flashcards.back: + Akkusativ: Ich bereite mich auf die Prüfung vor.

flashcards.front: Where does the verb go after 'weil'?
flashcards.back: To the END of the clause: Ich lerne, weil ich die Prüfung bestehen möchte.

flashcards.front: Where does the verb go after 'deshalb'?
flashcards.back: Position 2 (inversion): Deshalb lerne ich jeden Tag.

practice_sentences.de: Ich lerne jeden Tag Deutsch, __________ ich die Prüfung bestehen möchte.
practice_sentences.en: I study German every day because I want to pass the exam.

practice_sentences.de: __________ des schlechten Wetters bin ich zur Prüfung gegangen.
practice_sentences.en: Despite the bad weather, I went to the exam.
I am preparing for the test.

practice_sentences.de: Die Prüfung war schwer. __________ habe ich sie bestanden.
practice_sentences.en: The exam was hard. Nevertheless, I passed it.

practice_sentences.de: Es ist wichtig, __________ man regelmäßig übt.
practice_sentences.en: It's important that one practices regularly.

exam_tips.tip: Lesen Teil 2 (MCQ): Read the questions first, THEN the text. Underline the relevant sentence in the text for each question before choosing your answer.
Hören: You hear each recording only ONCE (Teil 1) or TWICE (Teil 2/3). Use the first listening for general understanding, second for specific answers.
Sprachbausteine: The most common tested items are: Konnektoren (weil/obwohl/damit/dass), Präpositionen mit Kasus, and Adjektivendungen. Master these three categories.
Schreiben: Start with 'Sehr geehrte Damen und Herren' or 'Liebe/r [Name]' depending on formality. End with 'Mit freundlichen Grüßen' (formal) or 'Viele Grüße' (semi-formal).
Sprechen: Prepare 5-6 sentences for your self-introduction (Name, Herkunft, Wohnort, Beruf/Studium, Familie, Hobbys). Practice until it flows naturally.

visual_summary.title: B1 Exam Section Strategies

rows: Focus on context, not every word
Slow and clear > fast and messy

key_points: Strategy matters as much as language skills
Always read questions/instructions BEFORE attempting the task
Don't leave blanks — always guess if unsure
Practice with real TELC mock exams for timing
Top 3 grammar areas: Konnektoren, Präpositionen+Kasus, Adjektivendungen
